threats.title: BotAdmin Forced Liquidation Fails Due to Incorrect Yield Account Resolution
threats.hypothesis_description: When the botAdmin attempts to liquidate an unhealthy yield position via _unstake or _repay, the contract incorrectly resolves the yield account using yieldAccounts[msg.sender] instead of the NFT owner's yield account, causing the liquidation to fail.
threats.investigation_rationale: The botAdmin is designed to force-liquidate positions that fall below the health factor threshold. By using msg.sender to look up the yield account, the bot's transactions will either revert (if the bot has no yield account) or execute against the wrong account, leaving the protocol with unliquidatable bad debt.
preconditions: A yield position's health factor drops below the unstakeHeathFactor
botAdmin attempts to call unstake or repay to liquidate the position

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Using msg.sender to resolve user-specific state in an admin-callable function
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: botAdmin is expected to perform liquidations but is blocked by this logic

operational_questions: Does the botAdmin have its own yield account?
Can the botAdmin successfully execute a liquidation end-to-end on testnet?
validation_requirements: Simulate a botAdmin call to unstake and repay for a user's NFT
Verify if the transaction reverts or affects the wrong account

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker opens a highly leveraged yield position
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker waits for the position's health factor to drop below the liquidation threshold
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: botAdmin attempts to liquidate but the transaction reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker's position remains open, accumulating bad debt for the protocol
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None, relies on protocol's inability to liquidate

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Protocol cannot liquidate unhealthy yield positions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires a contract upgrade to fix the yield account resolution logic

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic understanding of leverage and liquidation thresholds

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed transactions from the botAdmin address calling unstake or repay
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Positions with health factor < unstakeHeathFactor remaining active
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users intentionally over-leveraging knowing liquidations are broken

threats.title: Yield Borrowing Cap Bypass via Incorrect Index Multiplication
threats.hypothesis_description: YieldLogic.executeYieldBorrowERC20 calculates the total supply using groupData.borrowIndex instead of assetData.supplyIndex, inflating the perceived total supply and allowing yield borrowing to exceed the configured yieldCap.
threats.investigation_rationale: The yield cap is meant to limit the percentage of total deposits that can be borrowed for yield strategies. Because borrowIndex grows faster than supplyIndex, the calculated total supply is artificially inflated, progressively weakening the cap over time.
preconditions: borrowIndex is significantly higher than supplyIndex (which naturally occurs over time)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Passing a borrow index to a function that expects a supply index
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Always open, severity increases over time as indices diverge

operational_questions: How much does the borrowIndex diverge from the supplyIndex over time?
What is the actual maximum borrowable amount compared to the intended cap?
validation_requirements: Calculate the discrepancy between actual supply and inflated supply
Test borrowing up to the inflated cap

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for borrowIndex to grow significantly larger than supplyIndex
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Calculate the inflated total supply based on the bug
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Borrow funds for yield staking up to the inflated cap, exceeding the actual intended limit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Drain more liquidity from the lending pool than governance authorized

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Lending pool liquidity can be drained beyond safe limits, potentially causing insolvency or blocking normal withdrawals
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to fix the index usage
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Yield borrowing, lending pool liquidity management

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After protocol has been running long enough for indices to diverge

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Yield borrow totals exceeding the configured percentage of actual supply
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpectedly low available liquidity in the lending pool
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Maximized yield borrowing by whitelisted managers

threats.title: Intentional DoS of Yield Liquidation via WETH Approval Revocation
threats.hypothesis_description: In YieldStakingBase._repay, if the claimed yield is insufficient to cover the debt plus unstake fine, the contract attempts to pull the shortfall from the NFT owner. A malicious borrower can revoke WETH approval or empty their wallet, causing the botAdmin's liquidation transaction to revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: Liquidations must be guaranteed to execute regardless of the borrower's actions. Relying on the borrower's token approval and balance to cover a shortfall during a forced liquidation gives the borrower veto power over their own liquidation.
preconditions: A yield position is undercollateralized and being liquidated by botAdmin
The claimed yield from the external protocol is less than the debt + unstake fine

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Pulling funds from a user during a forced administrative action
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: botAdmin execution relies on user state
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When position is underwater and yield < debt + fine

operational_questions: What happens to the bad debt if the user refuses to pay the shortfall?
Is there a fallback mechanism to seize the NFT without requiring the shortfall payment?
validation_requirements: Simulate a liquidation where the user has 0 WETH balance

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker ensures their WETH allowance to the contract is 0, or their WETH balance is 0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Position becomes unhealthy and yield drops below debt + fine
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: botAdmin attempts to liquidate, but the safeTransferFrom reverts, blocking the liquidation

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Protocol cannot liquidate specific underwater positions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to handle shortfalls gracefully (e.g., seizing NFT instead of reverting)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed botAdmin transactions with ERC20 insufficient allowance/balance errors
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Underwater positions that remain open indefinitely
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users revoking approvals immediately after staking

threats.title: Healthy Isolate Loans Can Be Forced Into Auction
threats.hypothesis_description: ValidateLogic.validateIsolateAuctionLoan does not verify that the loan's health factor is below the liquidation threshold before allowing an auction to start. This could allow attackers to force healthy loans into auction.
threats.investigation_rationale: Auctions are punitive measures for undercollateralized loans. If the health factor is not checked when transitioning a loan from ACTIVE to AUCTION, attackers can grief users by forcing their healthy positions into auction, potentially extracting bid fines or acquiring NFTs at a discount.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing health factor check in validateIsolateAuctionLoan

preconditions: The executeIsolateAuction function (not fully provided but relies on ValidateLogic) does not independently check the health factor

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State transition to a punitive phase without verifying the punitive condition
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a user has an active isolate loan

operational_questions: Does executeIsolateAuction check the health factor independently?
Can a user lose their NFT or pay a fine if their healthy loan is auctioned?
validation_requirements: Review IsolateLiquidation.sol (Phase D) to confirm if HF is checked
Attempt to call isolateAuction on a loan with HF > 1

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a healthy isolate loan with valuable NFT collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The loan enters the AUCTION state despite being healthy
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker waits for auction duration and liquidates the NFT, or forces the owner to redeem and pay a bid fine

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users lose trust in the protocol as healthy positions are liquidated
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to add HF check

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Auctions starting for loans with HF > 1
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Specific addresses initiating auctions on healthy loans

threats.title: Dust Borrowing Evades Health Factor Checks via Rounding to Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: GenericLogic._getUserERC20DebtInBaseCurrency calculates debt in base currency using integer division. For small debt amounts, this truncates to zero. An attacker can open multiple small borrow positions across different groups, accumulating meaningful debt that is entirely ignored in health factor calculations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Health factor calculations must accurately reflect all outstanding debt. If debt can be hidden through rounding, users can bypass overcollateralization requirements, leading to undercollateralized positions that the protocol considers healthy.
preconditions: Asset price and decimals allow for a non-trivial amount of value to be rounded down to zero when divided by 10**decimals

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Integer division at the end of a value calculation without rounding up for debt

operational_questions: What is the maximum value that can be rounded to zero for supported assets?
Can an attacker open enough groups/positions to make the hidden debt economically significant?
validation_requirements: Calculate the rounding error bounds for WETH, USDT, DAI based on oracle prices
Simulate opening multiple dust positions and checking the aggregate health factor

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Calculate the maximum borrow amount that rounds to 0 in base currency for a specific asset
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Deposit a tiny amount of collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Borrow the calculated dust amount across multiple groups (e.g., low, middle, high rate groups)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The total actual debt exceeds the collateral value, but the calculated debt is 0, keeping HF at infinity

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to round up debt calculations
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Cross margin borrowing, health factor calculations

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Accounts with multiple tiny borrow positions across different groups
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Discrepancy between sum of individual debts and total tracked debt

threats.title: Stale Chainlink Oracle Prices Accepted Due to Missing Timestamp Check
threats.hypothesis_description: PriceOracle.getAssetPriceFromChainlink checks that updatedAt != 0 and answeredInRound >= roundId, but fails to enforce a maximum age for the price update. If a Chainlink feed stalls, the protocol will continue using the stale price.
threats.investigation_rationale: Oracles can occasionally stall or stop updating during network congestion or extreme volatility. Without a staleness threshold (e.g., block.timestamp - updatedAt <= MAX_DELAY), the protocol will value collateral incorrectly, allowing attackers to borrow against overvalued assets.
preconditions: A Chainlink aggregator stops updating its price feed
The actual market price of the asset drops significantly below the stale oracle price

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing block.timestamp comparison in oracle validation
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During oracle outages or extreme network congestion

operational_questions: What are the heartbeat configurations for the deployed Chainlink feeds?
Is there any off-chain monitoring to pause the protocol if feeds stall?
validation_requirements: Review PriceOracle implementation for any hidden staleness checks
Simulate an oracle outage and verify borrowing is still possible

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor Chainlink feeds for outages or delays
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Identify an asset whose market price has crashed but oracle price is stale and high
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Deposit the overvalued asset as collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Borrow stable assets against the stale collateral value, effectively selling the asset above market price

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Protocol acquires bad debt as collateral is worth less than borrowed funds
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires emergency pause and contract upgrade to add staleness checks

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large borrows occurring during periods of high volatility or known oracle delays
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Collateral deposits of assets that are crashing in external markets
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Exploitative borrowing patterns during network stress

threats.title: Yield Account Underflow Blocks Subsequent Stakes
threats.hypothesis_description: In YieldSavingsDai.getAccountTotalUnstakedYield, the contract subtracts accountYieldInWithdraws from the current balance. If the external yield token balance drops (e.g., due to DSR reduction or slashing), this subtraction will underflow, reverting the transaction and permanently blocking new stakes.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol assumes that the external yield balance will always be greater than or equal to the amount currently being withdrawn. If the external protocol experiences a loss, this assumption breaks, causing an underflow that bricks core functionality for that user.
preconditions: User has an active unstake request (inWithdraw > 0)
The external yield protocol (e.g., MakerDAO sDAI) experiences a decrease in share value or balance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unchecked subtraction between a dynamic external balance and a static internal accounting variable
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When external yield protocols experience losses

operational_questions: Can sDAI or other supported yield tokens experience negative rebases or share value drops?
Does this underflow affect other strategies like Lido or EtherFi?
validation_requirements: Simulate a drop in external yield balance while an unstake is pending

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User initiates an unstake, setting accountYieldInWithdraws
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: External protocol experiences a loss, reducing the user's yield account balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User attempts to stake another NFT using the same yield account
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The stake transaction reverts due to underflow in getAccountTotalUnstakedYield

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Specific users are permanently blocked from using their yield accounts for new stakes
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to handle underflow gracefully

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed stake transactions with arithmetic underflow errors
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users unable to interact with the protocol after an unstake

threats.title: Admin Can Drain All Yield Accounts via Rescue Function
threats.hypothesis_description: YieldRegistry.rescue allows the POOL_ADMIN to execute arbitrary calls from any YieldAccount via target.functionCall(data). A compromised or malicious admin can use this to transfer all staked assets out of every user's yield account.
threats.investigation_rationale: While rescue functions are common for recovering stuck funds, allowing arbitrary execution from user-owned accounts holding active collateral represents a massive centralization risk. A single compromised admin key can result in the total loss of all staked assets.
deployment_assumptions: POOL_ADMIN role is held by an EOA or a multisig that can be compromised
threats.config_evidence: DeployPoolFull.s.sol assigns POOL_ADMIN to deployer EOA: aclManager.addPoolAdmin(deployer);
preconditions: Attacker gains access to the POOL_ADMIN private key or compromises the admin multisig

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Arbitrary execution capability granted to an admin on user-owned contracts
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Always open if admin key is compromised

operational_questions: Is the POOL_ADMIN a timelocked multisig or an EOA?
Are there any off-chain monitoring systems to detect unauthorized rescue calls?
validation_requirements: Review deployment scripts to confirm POOL_ADMIN setup
Simulate an admin draining a yield account using the rescue function

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker compromises the POOL_ADMIN key via phishing, social engineering, or technical exploit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls YieldRegistry.rescue for every active YieldAccount
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The data payload executes a transfer of stETH, eETH, or sDAI to the attacker's address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All yield accounts are drained, leaving the protocol with massive uncollateralized debt
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires off-chain compromise of admin infrastructure

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total loss of user staked assets, protocol insolvency
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible to recover stolen funds without a chain fork or external bailout

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced social engineering or operational security exploitation

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden transfer of assets out of YieldAccounts not initiated by users
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Admin address executing unusual batch operations
investigation_priorities.detection_difficulty: low (easy to detect, hard to prevent once initiated)
